Equals(object?) Indicates whether this instance and a specified object are equal. public override bool Equals(object? obj) Parameters obj object The object to compare with the current instance. Returns bool true if obj and this instance are the same type and represent the same value; otherwise, false. Equals(GeoPoint<T>) Determines whether the specified GeoPoint<T> represents the same location as this instance, comparing latitude and longitude after rounding both to Utils.Geography.Model.GeoPoint<T>.EqualityPrecision decimal places. public bool Equals(GeoPoint<T> other) Parameters other GeoPoint<T> The point to compare with this instance. Returns bool true if both points round to the same latitude and longitude (or are both at the same pole, regardless of longitude); otherwise false. Remarks Why rounding, and not a tolerance window: latitude/longitude are floating-point values produced by trigonometric computations (Travel(T), Intersections(GeoVector<T>), ...), which never land on exact values. An earlier implementation compared raw values with a tolerance window (|a - b| <= 1e-5) via FloatingPointComparer<T>, but that comparer is intentionally non-transitive (see its own documentation) and cannot be paired with a correct GetHashCode(): two values considered equal by the tolerance window could still hash differently, breaking Dictionary<TKey, TValue>/HashSet<T> usage. Rounding both operands to the same precision before comparing removes that risk entirely, because Equals(GeoPoint<T>) and GetHashCode() then always operate on the exact same rounded values. Known limitation — rounding-boundary straddling: this trades away the "equal within a tolerance window" behavior. Two values that are extremely close to each other, but fall on opposite sides of a rounding boundary, are now treated as unequal, even though a raw tolerance comparison would have called them equal. For example, with Utils.Geography.Model.GeoPoint<T>.EqualityPrecision = 5, 1.0000449999 rounds down to 1.00004 while 1.0000450001 rounds up to 1.00005 — a difference of about 2e-7, far below the old 1e-5 tolerance, yet the two values are no longer equal. This is a deliberate, accepted trade-off (values this close in practice only arise from floating-point noise around a rounding boundary, and rounding-consistency was judged more valuable than tolerance-window equality for this type). See GeoPointTests.PointsOnOppositeSidesOfARoundingBoundaryAreNotEqual for a regression test that pins down this exact behavior. Longitude wraps around, latitude doesn't: longitude is compared via AreEqualRounded(T, T, int) rather than a plain rounded comparison, so that values on opposite sides of the antimeridian (e.g. 179.9999951° and -179.9999999°, which both refer to almost the same point near 180°) round to the same normalized value instead of comparing as ~360° apart. Latitude never wraps (it is clamped to [-90°, 90°] and the poles are handled separately above), so it only needs a plain rounded comparison. GetHashCode() Returns a hash code consistent with Equals(GeoPoint<T>): latitude is rounded, and longitude is normalized (to handle antimeridian wraparound) and rounded, to Utils.Geography.Model.GeoPoint<T>.EqualityPrecision decimal places before hashing — the exact same values that Equals(GeoPoint<T>) compares on, so equal points always hash equally. public override int GetHashCode() Returns int IsApproximately(GeoPoint<T>) Determines whether this point is within the default tolerance (see Utils.Geography.Model.GeoPoint<T>.comparer) of other. See IsApproximately(GeoPoint<T>, T) for the full rationale and usage guidance. public bool IsApproximately(GeoPoint<T> other) Parameters other GeoPoint<T> The point to compare with this instance. Returns bool true if both points are within the default tolerance; otherwise false. IsApproximately(GeoPoint<T>, T) Determines whether this point is within tolerance of other, using a raw angular-distance tolerance window rather than the rounding-based comparison used by Equals(GeoPoint<T>). public bool IsApproximately(GeoPoint<T> other, T tolerance) Parameters other GeoPoint<T> The point to compare with this instance. tolerance T Maximum allowed angular distance, in degrees, for latitude and longitude. Returns bool true if both latitude and longitude are within tolerance of each other (or both points are at the same pole); otherwise false. Remarks Equals(GeoPoint<T>) intentionally does not offer this behavior anymore (see its remarks): a tolerance window cannot be paired with a correct GetHashCode() because it is non-transitive, so points must not be considered equal, hashed, or stored in a Dictionary<TKey, TValue>/HashSet<T> based on this method. Use it only for one-off comparisons (e.g. "is this GPS fix close enough to the target waypoint?"), never as a substitute for Equals(GeoPoint<T>) in code that also relies on hashing. Longitude comparison correctly handles the antimeridian wraparound via AreEqual(T, T, T) (e.g. 179.9999999° and -179.9999999° are about 2e-7° apart, not ~360° apart). api/Utils.Geography.Model.GeoVector-1.ht.summary: Struct GeoVector<T> Namespace Utils.Geography.Model Assembly Utils.Geography.dll Represents a vector of displacement on a spherical geodesic with a bearing (heading direction). public readonly struct GeoVector<T> : IEquatable<GeoVector<T>>, IFormattable, IUnaryNegationOperators<GeoVector<T>, GeoVector<T>>, IEqualityOperators<GeoVector<T>, GeoVector<T>, bool> where T : struct, IFloatingPointIeee754<T>, IDivisionOperators<T, T, T> Type Parameters T The numeric type used for calculations, typically a floating point. Besides being the more accurate model, this also sidesteps a real bug the previous inheritance-based design had: Equals(object?) matches any GeoPoint<T>-derived instance via obj is GeoPoint<T>, so comparing a bare GeoPoint<T> against a GeoVector<T> at the same coordinates used to report them as equal (silently ignoring the bearing) while their hash codes (one over lat/lon, the other over lat/lon/bearing) could differ — breaking the GetHashCode() contract for any Dictionary<TKey, TValue>/HashSet<T> mixing both types. Composition makes that mismatch impossible: GeoVector<T> and GeoPoint<T> are unrelated types and can no longer compare equal to each other. Constructors GeoVector(string, params CultureInfo[]) Creates a GeoVector<T> from a single string containing latitude, longitude, and bearing. public GeoVector(string coordinates, params CultureInfo[] cultureInfos) Parameters coordinates string A string with three parts (latitude, longitude, bearing) separated by the culture's list separator. cultureInfos CultureInfo[] Optional cultures to parse; defaults to CurrentCulture and InvariantCulture. Exceptions ArgumentException Thrown if parsing fails or the string is invalid. GeoVector(string, string, T, params CultureInfo[]) Creates a GeoVector<T> from string representations of latitude, longitude, and a numeric bearing. public GeoVector(string latitudeString, string longitudeString, T bearing, params CultureInfo[] cultureInfos) Parameters latitudeString string Latitude string. longitudeString string Longitude string. bearing T Bearing direction in degrees. cultureInfos CultureInfo[] Culture information used to parse the coordinates. GeoVector(GeoPoint<T>, GeoPoint<T>) Creates a GeoVector<T> from two geographic points, calculating the bearing from geoPoint to destination. public GeoVector(GeoPoint<T> geoPoint, GeoPoint<T> destination) Parameters geoPoint GeoPoint<T> Start point. destination GeoPoint<T> Destination point. GeoVector(GeoPoint<T>, T) Creates a GeoVector<T> from a geoPoint plus a given bearing. public GeoVector(GeoPoint<T> geoPoint, T bearing) Parameters geoPoint GeoPoint<T> Base geographic point (latitude/longitude). bearing T Heading direction in degrees. GeoVector(T, T, T) Creates a GeoVector<T> from numeric latitude, longitude, and bearing. public GeoVector(T latitude, T longitude, T bearing) Parameters latitude T Latitude in degrees. longitude T Longitude in degrees. bearing T Heading direction in degrees. Properties Bearing Bearing in degrees relative to north, measured clockwise. Immutable. public T Bearing { get; } Property Value T Latitude Latitude in degrees (immutable). Alias for Point's latitude. public T Latitude { get; } Property Value T Longitude Longitude in degrees (immutable). Alias for Point's longitude. public T Longitude { get; } Property Value T Point The geographic position this vector originates from. Immutable. public GeoPoint<T> Point { get; } Property Value GeoPoint<T> θ Bearing in degrees relative to north, measured clockwise. This is an alias for the Bearing property. public T θ { get; } Property Value T λ Alias for Longitude. public T λ { get; } Property Value T φ Alias for Latitude. public T φ { get; } Property Value T Methods ComputeBearing(GeoPoint<T>, GeoPoint<T>) Computes the bearing from point A to point B in degrees. public static T ComputeBearing(GeoPoint<T> A, GeoPoint<T> B) Parameters A GeoPoint<T> Start point. B GeoPoint<T> Destination point. Returns T The bearing from A to B, normalized to [0..360). Deconstruct(out T, out T, out T) Deconstructs the GeoVector<T> into latitude, longitude, and bearing. public void Deconstruct(out T latitude, out T longitude, out T bearing) Parameters latitude T Latitude. longitude T Longitude. bearing T Bearing, in degrees. Equals(object?) Indicates whether this instance and a specified object are equal. public override bool Equals(object? obj) Parameters obj object The object to compare with the current instance. Returns bool true if obj and this instance are the same type and represent the same value; otherwise, false. Equals(GeoVector<T>) Determines whether the specified GeoVector<T> has the same position and bearing as this instance: latitude/longitude are compared via Equals(GeoPoint<T>) (rounded to Utils.Geography.Model.GeoPoint<T>.EqualityPrecision decimal places), and bearing is compared the same way longitude is: normalized and rounded to the same precision via AreEqualRounded(T, T, int), since bearing wraps around at 0°/360° just like longitude wraps around at the antimeridian. public bool Equals(GeoVector<T> other) Parameters other GeoVector<T> The vector to compare with this instance. Returns bool true if both vectors round to the same latitude, longitude, and bearing; otherwise false. Remarks This has the exact same known limitation as Equals(GeoPoint<T>) (see its remarks for the full rationale and a worked example): rounding each coordinate before comparing keeps Equals(GeoVector<T>) and GetHashCode() always consistent with each other, but two bearings that are extremely close yet fall on opposite sides of a rounding boundary (e.g. 1.0000449999 vs 1.0000450001, with Utils.Geography.Model.GeoPoint<T>.EqualityPrecision = 5) are treated as unequal. See GeoVectorTests.VectorsOnOppositeSidesOfARoundingBoundaryAreNotEqual for a regression test pinning down this behavior. GetHashCode() Returns a hash code consistent with Equals(GeoVector<T>): it combines Point's own hash code (already rounded/normalized, see GetHashCode()) with the bearing, normalized (to handle 0°/360° wraparound) and rounded to Utils.Geography.Model.GeoPoint<T>.EqualityPrecision decimal places — the exact same values that Equals(GeoVector<T>) compares on. public override int GetHashCode() Returns int Intersections(GeoVector<T>) Calculates intersection points between two great circles defined by this GeoVector<T> and the other one. public GeoPoint<T>[] Intersections(GeoVector<T> other) Parameters other GeoVector<T> The second vector/great circle. Returns GeoPoint<T>[] An array of 2 intersection points, or an empty array if the circles are identical. IsApproximately(GeoVector<T>) Determines whether this vector is within the default tolerance of other. See IsApproximately(GeoVector<T>, T) for the full rationale and usage guidance. public bool IsApproximately(GeoVector<T> other) Parameters other GeoVector<T> The vector to compare with this instance. Returns bool true if both vectors are within the default tolerance; otherwise false. IsApproximately(GeoVector<T>, T) Determines whether this vector is within tolerance of other, using a raw angular-distance tolerance window rather than the rounding-based comparison used by Equals(GeoVector<T>). See IsApproximately(GeoPoint<T>, T) for the full rationale and usage guidance (do not use this for hashing/dictionary keys). public bool IsApproximately(GeoVector<T> other, T tolerance) Parameters other GeoVector<T> The vector to compare with this instance. tolerance T Maximum allowed angular distance, in degrees, for latitude, longitude, and bearing. Returns bool true if latitude, longitude, and bearing are all within tolerance of each other; otherwise false. Recenter(GeoVector<T>) Returns a new GeoVector such that: The current vector ("this") is mapped to (0°, 0°, 0°). The new latitude = spherical distance (central angle) from "this" to "other". The new longitude = difference in initial bearings, so that "this.Bearing" becomes 0°. The new bearing = difference in heading from "this.Bearing". Change the reference so that the current vector is effectively the new origin with heading=0. public GeoVector<T> Recenter(GeoVector<T> other) Parameters other GeoVector<T> Returns GeoVector<T> The point recentered with the current vector as new reference ToString() Returns this geographic vector as a string in the format "Latitude, Longitude, Bearing" with five decimal places by default. public override string ToString() Returns string ToString(string) Returns this geographic vector as a string in the specified format and the invariant culture. public string ToString(string format) Parameters format string Returns string ToString(string?, IFormatProvider?) Returns a string representation of the vector using the specified format and provider. public string ToString(string? format, IFormatProvider? formatProvider) Parameters format string Format string applied to latitude, longitude, and bearing. formatProvider IFormatProvider Culture-specific format provider. Returns string A formatted string representing the vector. Travel(T) Calculates the resulting vector after traveling a certain angular distance from this vector’s position. public GeoVector<T> Travel(T angle) Parameters angle T Angular distance to travel (in degrees). Returns GeoVector<T> A new GeoVector<T> representing the position and bearing after traveling. Initializes a new instance of the Planet<T> class. public Planet(T equatorialRadius, string? name = null) Parameters equatorialRadius T The equatorial radius of the planet. name string The name of the planet (optional). Properties EquatorialCircumference The equatorial circumference of the planet. public T EquatorialCircumference { get; } Property Value T EquatorialRadius The equatorial radius of the planet. public T EquatorialRadius { get; } Property Value T Name The name of the planet. public string Name { get; } Property Value string Methods Area(IReadOnlyList<GeoPoint<T>>) Calculates the surface area of a spherical polygon defined by a list of geographic points. public T Area(IReadOnlyList<GeoPoint<T>> points) Parameters points IReadOnlyList<GeoPoint<T>> The polygon vertices in order. The first and last point need not be equal. Returns T The area of the polygon in square meters. Remarks This uses the standard "sum of longitude differences weighted by latitude" spherical excess formula, connecting consecutive vertices with great-circle arcs. It is well-suited to typical, reasonably-sized, simple (non-self-intersecting) polygons, but has known limitations: A polygon that encloses a pole (crosses from one side of it to the other without the pole itself being a vertex) is not detected as a special case; the result may be the area of the polygon or of its complement on the sphere, depending on winding, rather than throwing or otherwise flagging the ambiguity. Self-intersecting polygons do not produce a meaningful area (as with the planar shoelace formula). For polygons that span a very large fraction of the planet's surface, the flat "longitude difference" term becomes a coarser approximation of the true spherical geometry. Exceptions ArgumentNullException Thrown when points is null. ArgumentException Thrown when fewer than three points are supplied. Distance(GeoPoint<T>, GeoPoint<T>) Calculates the distance between two geographical points on the planet's surface. public T Distance(GeoPoint<T> geoPoint1, GeoPoint<T> geoPoint2) Parameters geoPoint1 GeoPoint<T> The first geographical point. geoPoint2 GeoPoint<T> The second geographical point. Returns T The distance between the two points in meters. LatitudeDistance(T) Calculates the degrees of latitude that correspond to a specified distance on the planet's surface. public T LatitudeDistance(T meters) Parameters meters T The distance in meters. LongitudeDistance(T, T) Calculates the degrees of longitude that correspond to a specified distance at a given latitude. public T LongitudeDistance(T meters, T latitude) Parameters meters T The distance in meters. latitude T The latitude at which the calculation should be performed. Returns T The degrees of longitude corresponding to the given distance at the specified latitude. Travel(GeoVector<T>, T) Travels a given distance along a geodesic path starting from the provided vector. public GeoVector<T> Travel(GeoVector<T> geoVector, T distance) Parameters geoVector GeoVector<T> The starting vector. distance T The distance to travel in meters. Returns GeoVector<T> A new GeoVector<T> representing the destination point.

Used by Normalize(ProjectedPoint<T>) (and, in turn, by MapPoint<T> and RepresentationConverter<T>) to convert a projected point into map-fraction coordinates before scaling to a pixel/tile grid. public (T MinX, T MaxX, T MinY, T MaxY) Bounds { get; } Property Value (T MinX, T MaxX, T MinY, T MaxY) Remarks For a projection whose output is genuinely unbounded at some point of the sphere (e.g. Mercator at the poles), Bounds must report a practical, finite envelope rather than the true (infinite) mathematical range — see each projection's own documentation for how that envelope is chosen and what it excludes. This member has a default implementation (throwing NotSupportedException) rather than being required, so that adding it to this public interface does not break existing third-party IProjectionTransformation<T> implementations: they keep compiling and working exactly as before, and only fail — with a clear message — if they (or code that consumes them, like Normalize(ProjectedPoint<T>), MapPoint<T>'s ProjectedPoint<T> constructor, or MappointToTile(ProjectedPoint<T>, byte)) actually try to use it without overriding it. All 7 projections built into this package override it. api/Utils.Geography.Projections.GallsPet.summary: Class GallsPetersProjection<T> Namespace Utils.Geography.Projections Assembly Utils.Geography.dll Implements the Gall–Peters cylindrical equal-area projection "centered" at (lat=0°, lon=0°) => (x=0, y=0). Formulas (in degrees): x = cos(45°) * longitude y = sin(latitude) / cos(45°) lon = x / cos(45°) lat = asin( y * cos(45°) ) Both input (GeoPoint) and output (ProjectedPoint) use degrees. public class GallsPetersProjection<T> : IProjectionTransformation<T> where T : struct, IFloatingPointIeee754<T> Type Parameters T A numeric type implementing IFloatingPointIeee754 (e.g. float, double, decimal). (T MinX, T MaxX, T MinY, T MaxY) Bounds { get; } Property Value (T MinX, T MaxX, T MinY, T MaxY) Remarks For a projection whose output is genuinely unbounded at some point of the sphere (e.g. Mercator at the poles), Bounds must report a practical, finite envelope rather than the true (infinite) mathematical range — see each projection's own documentation for how that envelope is chosen and what it excludes. This member has a default implementation (throwing NotSupportedException) rather than being required, so that adding it to this public interface does not break existing third-party IProjectionTransformation<T> implementations: they keep compiling and working exactly as before, and only fail — with a clear message — if they (or code that consumes them, like Normalize(ProjectedPoint<T>), MapPoint<T>'s ProjectedPoint<T> constructor, or MappointToTile(ProjectedPoint<T>, byte)) actually try to use it without overriding it. All 7 projections built into this package override it. Methods GeoPointToMapPoint(GeoPoint<T>) Transforms a geographic point (latitude/longitude) into a projected map point. ProjectedPoint<T> GeoPointToMapPoint(GeoPoint<T> geoPoint) Parameters geoPoint GeoPoint<T> A geographic point. Returns ProjectedPoint<T> The corresponding projected point on a 2D map. MapPointToGeoPoint(ProjectedPoint<T>) Transforms a projected map point back into a geographic point (latitude/longitude). GeoPoint<T> MapPointToGeoPoint(ProjectedPoint<T> mapPoint) Parameters mapPoint ProjectedPoint<T> A projected point on a 2D map. Returns GeoPoint<T> The corresponding geographic point. Normalize(ProjectedPoint<T>) Normalizes projectedPoint into map-fraction coordinates using Bounds: (MinX, MinY) maps to (0, 0) and (MaxX, MaxY) maps to (1, 1), independently on each axis. (T X, T Y) Normalize(ProjectedPoint<T> projectedPoint) Parameters projectedPoint ProjectedPoint<T> A point produced by GeoPointToMapPoint(GeoPoint<T>) using this projection. Returns (T X, T Y) The normalized (X, Y) coordinates. For points within Bounds these are in [0, 1]; a point outside Bounds (e.g. near an excluded singularity, see its remarks) normalizes outside that range rather than being clamped. Remarks This is a plain independent linear rescale of each axis: it does not assume that either axis corresponds to a cardinal direction (that assumption only holds for cylindrical/pseudo-cylindrical projections such as MercatorProjection<T>/EquirectangularProjection<T>, not for azimuthal ones such as LambertAzimuthalEqualArea<T>/StereographicProjection<T> where a single axis does not track latitude alone). Callers that need a specific tile-numbering orientation (e.g. row 0 = north, matching common slippy-map conventions) must apply that convention themselves on top of this normalized value.

All 7 projections built into this package override it. MaxLatitude Gets the maximum (and, by symmetry, minimum negated) latitude this projection treats as representable: ~85.05112878°, the standard "Web Mercator" cutoff used by essentially every slippy-map implementation (OpenStreetMap, Google Maps, Bing Maps, Leaflet, ...). It is the solution of 2·atan(e^π) - π/2, i.e. the latitude at which this projection's Y value (converted to radians) equals π. public static T MaxLatitude { get; } Property Value T Remarks Unlike latitude itself, Y = ln(tan(45° + lat/2)) grows without bound as latitude approaches ±90° (the poles are a genuine mathematical singularity for Mercator: tan(90°) is undefined). Bounds therefore cannot use lat=±90° directly and instead evaluates Y at MaxLatitude, giving a finite, standard, and widely-recognized envelope. X is treated as longitude, Y as ln(tan(45 + lat/2)). public GeoPoint<T> MapPointToGeoPoint(ProjectedPoint<T> mapPoint) Parameters mapPoint ProjectedPoint<T> Projected point with X=lon, Y=ln(tan(45+lat/2)). Returns GeoPoint<T> A geographic point in degrees.

Lat/Lon in degrees => (x,y) in "Mollweide units" with: (0°,0°) maps to (0,0). The bounding ellipse is roughly x ∈ [-2√2..+2√2], y ∈ [-√2..+√2]. Usage: var projector = new MollweideProjectionDegrees<double>(); var projected = projector.GeoPointToMapPoint(new GeoPoint<double>(latDeg, lonDeg)); var unprojected = projector.MapPointToGeoPoint(projected); public class MollweideProjection<T> : IProjectionTransformation<T> where T : struct, IFloatingPointIeee754<T> Type Parameters T A floating point type implementing IFloatingPointIeee754, e.g. float/double/decimal. All 7 projections built into this package override it. Methods GeoPointToMapPoint(GeoPoint<T>) Projects (latitude, longitude) in degrees => Mollweide (x, y). The Newton solve and trigonometry are carried out in radians (the mathematically correct unit for the classic Mollweide equation); latitude/longitude are only converted to/from degrees at the boundary of this method. Forward formula: (1) Solve 2θ + sin(2θ) = π * sin(lat), for θ ∈ [-π/2..+π/2]. (2) x = (2√2 / π) * lon * cos(θ) (3) y = √2 * sin(θ) public ProjectedPoint<T> GeoPointToMapPoint(GeoPoint<T> geoPoint) Parameters geoPoint GeoPoint<T> Returns ProjectedPoint<T> MapPointToGeoPoint(ProjectedPoint<T>) Unprojects (x,y) in Mollweide => (lat, lon) in degrees. Inverse (all computed in radians, converted to degrees only for the result): (1) θ = asin( y / √2 ) (2) lon = x * (π / 2√2 cos(θ)) (3) lat = asin( [2θ + sin(2θ)] / π ) public GeoPoint<T> MapPointToGeoPoint(ProjectedPoint<T> mapPoint) Parameters mapPoint ProjectedPoint<T> Returns GeoPoint<T>

GeoPointToMapPoint(GeoPoint<T>) does not throw at that point; instead it substitutes T.Epsilon (the smallest positive value representable by T) for the zero denominator, which produces an extremely large but finite k factor and, in turn, extremely large (not NaN, but potentially positive infinity after overflow) x/y values rather than a clear error. Callers that need points near the antipodal meridian should check for this case explicitly (e.g. degree.AreEqual(geoPoint.Latitude, 0, tolerance) && degree.AreEqual(System.Math.Abs(geoPoint.Longitude), 180, tolerance)) rather than relying on the output being finite and meaningful. Properties Bounds Gets the rectangular bounds, expressed in this projection's own native output units, that GeoPointToMapPoint(GeoPoint<T>) stays within for every valid GeoPoint<T>. Used by Normalize(ProjectedPoint<T>) (and, in turn, by MapPoint<T> and RepresentationConverter<T>) to convert a projected point into map-fraction coordinates before scaling to a pixel/tile grid. public (T MinX, T MaxX, T MinY, T MaxY) Bounds { get; } Property Value (T MinX, T MaxX, T MinY, T MaxY) Remarks For a projection whose output is genuinely unbounded at some point of the sphere (e.g. Mercator at the poles), Bounds must report a practical, finite envelope rather than the true (infinite) mathematical range — see each projection's own documentation for how that envelope is chosen and what it excludes. This member has a default implementation (throwing NotSupportedException) rather than being required, so that adding it to this public interface does not break existing third-party IProjectionTransformation<T> implementations: they keep compiling and working exactly as before, and only fail — with a clear message — if they (or code that consumes them, like Normalize(ProjectedPoint<T>), MapPoint<T>'s ProjectedPoint<T> constructor, or MappointToTile(ProjectedPoint<T>, byte)) actually try to use it without overriding it. All 7 projections built into this package override it. Methods GeoPointToMapPoint(GeoPoint<T>) Projects geographic coordinates onto the stereographic projection plane. public ProjectedPoint<T> GeoPointToMapPoint(GeoPoint<T> geoPoint) Parameters geoPoint GeoPoint<T> Geographic coordinates in degrees. Returns ProjectedPoint<T> Projected map coordinates. MapPointToGeoPoint(ProjectedPoint<T>) Converts stereographic projection coordinates back to geographic coordinates. public GeoPoint<T> MapPointToGeoPoint(ProjectedPoint<T> mapPoint) Parameters mapPoint ProjectedPoint<T> Projected map coordinates. Returns GeoPoint<T> Geographic coordinates in degrees.

api/Utils.Geography.Projections.html.title: Namespace Utils.Geography.Projections | omy.Utils Documentation
api/Utils.Geography.Projections.html.summary: Namespace Utils.Geography.Projections Classes EquirectangularProjection<T> A simple Equirectangular (Plate Carrée) projection that uses degrees throughout: X = longitude (in degrees) Y = latitude (in degrees) Hence (0°, 0°) => (0,0) with no offsets or scalings. If latitude exceeds ±90, it is optionally clamped. GallsPetersProjection<T> Implements the Gall–Peters cylindrical equal-area projection "centered" at (lat=0°, lon=0°) => (x=0, y=0). Formulas (in degrees): x = cos(45°) * longitude y = sin(latitude) / cos(45°) lon = x / cos(45°) lat = asin( y * cos(45°) ) Both input (GeoPoint) and output (ProjectedPoint) use degrees. LambertAzimuthalEqualArea<T> Implements the polar aspect of the Lambert azimuthal equal-area projection for spherical coordinates: the projection is centered on the north pole (latitude=90°), which maps to (x=0, y=0), not on the equator/prime meridian. MercatorProjection<T> centered Mercator projection where (lat=0°, lon=0°) maps to (X=0, Y=0), Forward transform: X = lon Y = ln( tan(45° + lat/2) ) Inverse transform: lon = X lat = 2 * [ atan( exp(Y) ) - 45° ] MillerProjection<T> Implements the Miller cylindrical map projection. MollweideProjection<T> Mollweide (Homalographic) equal-area projection, implemented with degree-based trigonometry. Lat/Lon in degrees => (x,y) in "Mollweide units" with: (0°,0°) maps to (0,0). The bounding ellipse is roughly x ∈ [-2√2..+2√2], y ∈ [-√2..+√2]. Usage: var projector = new MollweideProjectionDegrees<double>(); var projected = projector.GeoPointToMapPoint(new GeoPoint<double>(latDeg, lonDeg)); var unprojected = projector.MapPointToGeoPoint(projected); ProjectionTransformation<T> Represents an abstract base class for map projection transformations that convert between geographic (GeoPoint<T>) and projected (ProjectedPoint<T>) coordinates. Projections<T> Provides cached access to a collection of well-known map projections. StereographicProjection<T> Implements the stereographic map projection for spherical coordinates, centered at (lat=0°, lon=0°). Interfaces IProjectionTransformation<T> Represents a generic transformation interface that converts GeoPoint<T> objects to ProjectedPoint<T> objects and vice versa.

When false, filler characters are silently ignored everywhere (legacy behaviour). Properties BaseDescriptor Gets the descriptor that defines the base encoding alphabet. protected IBaseDescriptor BaseDescriptor { get; } Property Value IBaseDescriptor Encoding When overridden in a derived class, returns the character encoding in which the output is written. public override Encoding Encoding { get; } Property Value Encoding The character encoding in which the output is written. Stream Gets the target binary stream. public Stream Stream { get; } Property Value Stream Methods Close() Finalizes the decoding process and flushes remaining bits. After the first call (successful or not) the instance is permanently condemned: any subsequent Write(char) call throws ObjectDisposedException. A second call to Close() is always a no-op. TextWriter resources are released even when a FormatException is thrown. public override void Close() Exceptions FormatException Thrown in strict mode when the input length is incomplete, trailing bits are non-zero, or the padding count does not match the expected value. Flush() Clears all buffers for the current writer and causes any buffered data to be written to the underlying device. public override void Flush() Write(char) Writes a single base character to the decoder. public override void Write(char value) Parameters value char The encoded character. Exceptions ObjectDisposedException Thrown when writing after the decoder is closed. FormatException Thrown in strict mode when value is not a valid alphabet character, or padding appears in an illegal position.

api/Utils.IO.BaseEncoding.html.title: Namespace Utils.IO.BaseEncoding | omy.Utils Documentation
api/Utils.IO.BaseEncoding.html.summary: Namespace Utils.IO.BaseEncoding Classes BaseDecoderStream Writes base-encoded characters and produces binary output to an underlying Stream. By default the decoder operates in strict mode: characters outside the alphabet, misplaced padding and incomplete symbol groups are rejected. Pass strict = false to revert to the original permissive behaviour. BaseDescriptorBase Base implementation for IBaseDescriptor and IBaseConverter. BaseEncoderStream Stream that encodes written binary data into a base representation. Bases Provides predefined base encoding descriptors. Bases.Base16Descriptor Descriptor for base-16 (hexadecimal) encoding. Bases.Base32Descriptor Descriptor for base-32 encoding. Bases.Base64Descriptor Descriptor for base-64 encoding. Interfaces IBaseConverter Converts data to and from a textual base representation. IBaseDescriptor Describes a base encoding by exposing lookup tables and formatting options.

api/Utils.IO.PartialStream.html.title: Class PartialStream | omy.Utils Documentation
api/Utils.IO.PartialStream.html.summary: Class PartialStream Namespace Utils.IO Assembly Utils.IO.dll Provides a view onto a restricted portion (a "slice") of an underlying Stream. This partial stream starts at a specified offset within the base stream, and has a maximum length. Reading or writing beyond that partial length is not permitted. The Position property reflects the offset within the partial stream, not the offset in the underlying stream. All read and write operations are bounded by the specified length. The underlying stream's position is restored after each operation, so external consumers of the base stream see no position change (though locking is used to ensure thread safety in concurrent scenarios). By default, disposing this PartialStream does not close or dispose the underlying stream. public class PartialStream : Stream, IAsyncDisposable, IDisposable Inheritance object MarshalByRefObject ArgumentOutOfRangeException Thrown if length is negative or if the current stream position plus length would overflow a long. PartialStream(Stream, long, long) Creates a new partial stream starting at a specified position in the base stream. public PartialStream(Stream baseStream, long position, long length) Parameters baseStream Stream The underlying stream to read from or write to. position long The absolute position in baseStream at which to start. Must be non-negative. length long The length (in bytes) of the accessible segment. Must be non-negative. Exceptions ArgumentNullException Thrown if baseStream is null. ArgumentException Thrown if the baseStream cannot seek. ArgumentOutOfRangeException Thrown if position or length is negative, or if their sum would overflow a long. Properties CanRead Gets a value indicating whether the stream supports reading. public override bool CanRead { get; } Property Value bool CanSeek Gets a value indicating whether the stream supports seeking. public override bool CanSeek { get; } Property Value bool CanWrite Gets a value indicating whether the stream supports writing. public override bool CanWrite { get; } Property Value bool Length Gets the length of this partial view of the underlying stream. Setting this value does not change the length of the underlying stream. public override long Length { get; } Property Value long Position Gets or sets the position within this partial stream. Must be in [0, Length]. public override long Position { get; set; } Property Value long Exceptions ArgumentOutOfRangeException Thrown when the value is negative or exceeds Length. Methods Dispose(bool) Disposes the partial stream. By default, this does not close or dispose the underlying stream. If you need to dispose the base stream, do so externally. protected override void Dispose(bool disposing) Parameters disposing bool True to release both managed and unmanaged resources; false to release only unmanaged resources. Flush() Clears any buffers for this stream and causes any buffered data to be written to the underlying device. public override void Flush() Read(byte[], int, int) Reads a sequence of bytes from this partial stream and advances the position within this partial stream by the number of bytes read. public override int Read(byte[] buffer, int offset, int count) Parameters buffer byte[] The buffer to store the read bytes. offset int The byte offset in buffer at which to begin storing data. count int The maximum number of bytes to read. Returns int The total number of bytes read into buffer. This might be less than the number of bytes requested if that many bytes are not currently available, or if the read is constrained by the partial stream length. Seek(long, SeekOrigin) Sets the position within this partial stream. public override long Seek(long offset, SeekOrigin origin) Parameters offset long A byte offset relative to the origin parameter. origin SeekOrigin A value of type SeekOrigin indicating the reference point. Returns long The new position within this partial stream. SetLength(long) Sets the length of the partial stream. This does not affect the underlying stream length, but changes the maximum accessible range of this partial view. public override void SetLength(long value) Parameters value long The desired length of this partial stream. Exceptions ArgumentOutOfRangeException Thrown if value is negative or if startOffset + value would overflow a long. Write(byte[], int, int) Writes a sequence of bytes to this partial stream and advances the position within this partial stream by the number of bytes written. public override void Write(byte[] buffer, int offset, int count) Parameters buffer byte[] The buffer containing the data to write. offset int The zero-based byte offset in buffer at which to begin copying data. count int The number of bytes to write. Exceptions ArgumentOutOfRangeException Thrown if writing the specified number of bytes would exceed the Length of the partial stream.

api/Utils.IO.Serialization.html.summary: Namespace Utils.IO.Serialization Classes FieldAttribute Marks a field or property as part of the binary serialization process. GenerateReaderWriterAttribute Marks a type for which reader and writer methods are generated at compile time. RawReader Provides basic readers for primitive and common framework types. Each method reads a value using the supplied IReader. RawWriter Provides basic writers for primitive and common framework types. Each method writes a value using the supplied IWriter. Reader Generic reader capable of dynamically reading objects from a stream. ReaderWriter Helper class exposing a Reader and a Writer over the same stream. IStreamMapping<T> Represents a stream that supports reading, seeking and slicing operations. IWriter Provides methods for writing primitive and complex values to a stream.

api/Utils.IO.StreamCopier.html.title: Class StreamCopier | omy.Utils Documentation
api/Utils.IO.StreamCopier.html.summary: Class StreamCopier Namespace Utils.IO Assembly Utils.IO.dll A writable-only stream that copies written data to multiple target streams simultaneously. This class implements both Stream and IList<T> so that the set of target streams can be dynamically modified at runtime. Note that no concurrency control is provided; if multiple threads write concurrently, external locking may be needed. Reading and seeking are not supported. Calling Write(byte[], int, int) will broadcast the provided data to all underlying streams in the targets collection. Best-effort fan-out: Write(byte[], int, int) and Flush() attempt the operation on every target stream even if earlier ones throw. All exceptions are collected and rethrown as an AggregateException so no target is silently skipped. This means targets that were reached before a failure already hold the written data while later ones may not — callers that need strict all-or-nothing semantics must manage this externally. By default, disposing this object does not dispose any of the contained streams. If the parameter closeAllTargetsOnDispose is true when constructing this class, all target streams will be disposed when Dispose() is called. public class StreamCopier : Stream, IAsyncDisposable, If null, defaults to UTF8. Returns string A string containing all text read from the stream. ReadBlock(Stream, byte[]) Reads and returns the next "block" from the stream, delimited by the specified byte sequence. The returned array does not include the delimiter itself. If the delimiter is not found before EOF, all remaining bytes in the stream are returned. This method can be called repeatedly to read multiple blocks from the same stream. public static byte[] ReadBlock(this Stream s, byte[] blockSeparator) Parameters s Stream The Stream from which to read the block. blockSeparator byte[] A byte array representing the block delimiter. Returns byte[] A byte array containing the next block of data, not including the delimiter. If EOF is reached before finding the delimiter, returns all remaining data (which could be empty). Exceptions ArgumentNullException Thrown if blockSeparator is null. ArgumentException Thrown if blockSeparator is empty (cannot search for an empty delimiter). ReadBytes(Stream, int, bool) Reads exactly length bytes from the given Stream and returns them in a byte array. If false, returns only the bytes that were actually read. Returns byte[] A byte array of exactly length bytes when successful, or a shorter array when raiseException is false and the stream ends early. Exceptions EndOfStreamException Thrown if raiseException is true and the stream ends before length bytes could be read. ReadLines(Stream, Encoding) Reads line-by-line from a text-based stream until EOF. Each line is returned as an element in the resulting sequence. public static IEnumerable<string> ReadLines(this Stream s, Encoding encoding = null) Parameters s Stream The Stream to read from. encoding Encoding The character encoding to use. If null, defaults to UTF8. Returns IEnumerable<string> An IEnumerable<T> of lines read from the stream. The stream is read lazily; re-iterating this enumerable will not re-read the stream. ReadToEnd(Stream, int?) Reads all remaining data from the given Stream until EOF and returns it as a byte array. public static byte[] ReadToEnd(this Stream s, int? maxBytes = null) Parameters s Stream The source Stream to read from. maxBytes int? Optional maximum number of bytes to read. Throws InvalidOperationException if the stream contains more data than this limit. null means no limit. Returns byte[] Exceptions InvalidOperationException Thrown when maxBytes is exceeded. ReadToMemoryStream(Stream) Reads all remaining data from the current Stream and returns a new MemoryStream containing that data. public static MemoryStream ReadToMemoryStream(this Stream s) Parameters s Stream The source Stream to read from. api/Utils.IO.StreamValidator.html.title: Class StreamValidator | omy.Utils Documentation
api/Utils.IO.StreamValidator.html.summary: Class StreamValidator Namespace Utils.IO Assembly Utils.IO.dll A write-only buffering stream that defers writing to a target stream until Validate() is called. Data written to this stream is held in an internal buffer. When Validate() is invoked, the buffered data is flushed into the underlying target stream. If Discard() is called, the buffered data is discarded without being written to the target. This class does not support reading or seeking. Atomicity: Validate() is not atomic. If the underlying target throws mid-write, some bytes may already have been written. For seekable targets, only the stream position is restored on failure; the content and length of the target may already have been partially modified. The internal buffer is preserved after a failed Validate() so the caller can decide whether to call Discard() or to retry (bearing in mind that the target content is in an indeterminate state after a partial write). For non-seekable targets a partial write is irrecoverable; callers should treat the target as corrupted if Validate() throws. The underlying stream is not closed or disposed automatically when this stream is disposed. public class StreamValidator : Stream, IAsyncDisposable, IDisposable Inheritance object MarshalByRefObject api/Utils.IO.html.title: Namespace Utils.IO | omy.Utils Documentation
api/Utils.IO.html.summary: Namespace Utils.IO Classes PartialStream Provides a view onto a restricted portion (a "slice") of an underlying Stream. This partial stream starts at a specified offset within the base stream, and has a maximum length. Reading or writing beyond that partial length is not permitted. The Position property reflects the offset within the partial stream, not the offset in the underlying stream. All read and write operations are bounded by the specified length. The underlying stream's position is restored after each operation, so external consumers of the base stream see no position change (though locking is used to ensure thread safety in concurrent scenarios). By default, disposing this PartialStream does not close or dispose the underlying stream. StreamCopier A writable-only stream that copies written data to multiple target streams simultaneously. This class implements both Stream and IList<T> so that the set of target streams can be dynamically modified at runtime. Note that no concurrency control is provided; if multiple threads write concurrently, external locking may be needed. Reading and seeking are not supported. Calling Write(byte[], int, int) will broadcast the provided data to all underlying streams in the targets collection. Best-effort fan-out: Write(byte[], int, int) and Flush() attempt the operation on every target stream even if earlier ones throw. All exceptions are collected and rethrown as an AggregateException so no target is silently skipped. This means targets that were reached before a failure already hold the written data while later ones may not — callers that need strict all-or-nothing semantics must manage this externally. By default, disposing this object does not dispose any of the contained streams. If the parameter closeAllTargetsOnDispose is true when constructing this class, all target streams will be disposed when Dispose() is called. StreamUtils Provides extension and utility methods for working with Stream objects. StreamValidator A write-only buffering stream that defers writing to a target stream until Validate() is called. Data written to this stream is held in an internal buffer. When Validate() is invoked, the buffered data is flushed into the underlying target stream. If Discard() is called, the buffered data is discarded without being written to the target. This class does not support reading or seeking. Atomicity: Validate() is not atomic. If the underlying target throws mid-write, some bytes may already have been written. For seekable targets, only the stream position is restored on failure; the content and length of the target may already have been partially modified. The internal buffer is preserved after a failed Validate() so the caller can decide whether to call Discard() or to retry (bearing in mind that the target content is in an indeterminate state after a partial write). For non-seekable targets a partial write is irrecoverable; callers should treat the target as corrupted if Validate() throws. The underlying stream is not closed or disposed automatically when this stream is disposed.

api/Utils.Imaging.html.title: Namespace Utils.Imaging | omy.Utils Documentation
api/Utils.Imaging.html.summary: Namespace Utils.Imaging Classes BitmapAccessor Provides direct indexed access to a bitmap. BitmapArgb32Accessor Provides direct memory access to 32-bit ARGB bitmap data, enabling fast pixel manipulation scenarios. BitmapArgb64Accessor Provides direct memory access to 64-bit ARGB bitmap data for high precision pixel manipulation scenarios. BitmapIndexed8Accessor Provides direct memory access to 8-bit indexed bitmap data. ColorAhsv HSV color representation using double components. ColorAhsv32 HSV color stored with 8-bit components. ColorAhsv64 HSV color representation using 16-bit components. ColorBlend Provides color blending operations similar to those found in image editing software. ConvolutionMatrixFactory Provides common convolution kernels for image processing. ImageAccessorExtensions Provides helper methods for IImageAccessor<T> instances. ImageUtils Provides helpers to resolve image encoders and decoders by MIME type. MatrixImageTransformer<A, T> Applies a weighted matrix transformation to an image. Structs ColorArgb Represents an ARGB color using double components. ColorArgb32 Represents a 32-bit ARGB color using byte components. ColorArgb64 Represents a 64-bit ARGB color using 16-bit components. Interfaces IColorAhsv<T> Represents a color value expressed through alpha, hue, saturation, and value channels. IColorArgbConvertible<TSelf, TArgb, T> Defines a color system that can convert to and from an ARGB representation. IColorArgb<T> Represents a color value expressed through alpha, red, green, and blue channels. IImageAccessor<T> Describes an abstraction capable of exposing read/write access to a two-dimensional image resource. IImageAccessor<A, T> Provides strongly typed access to an image whose pixels are represented by an ARGB color structure. IImageTransformer<A, T> Defines a generic transformation that can be applied to an image.

Unlike the name-based constructor, this identifies the differentiation variable unambiguously even when parameter's Name is null or shared by another, unrelated parameter in the same lambda (see TODO-2026-07-11-pass4.md item #47). public ExpressionDerivation(ParameterExpression parameter, bool allowNumericalFallback = false) Parameters parameter ParameterExpression The exact parameter instance to differentiate against. allowNumericalFallback bool See AllowNumericalFallback. ExpressionDerivation(string, bool) Initializes a new instance of the ExpressionDerivation<T> class for the specified parameter. public ExpressionDerivation(string parameterName, bool allowNumericalFallback = false) Parameters parameterName string Name of the variable with respect to which derivatives are computed. allowNumericalFallback bool See AllowNumericalFallback. Defaults to false: unknown methods fail explicitly instead of silently falling back to a numerical approximation. Properties AllowNumericalFallback Gets whether unknown single-argument methods (with no registered symbolic derivative rule) may be differentiated through a centered finite-difference approximation (see DeriveUnknownMethodCall(MethodCallExpression, Expression[])) instead of failing with NotSupportedException. This is opt-in and defaults to false, since the fallback turns an exact symbolic derivation into a numerical runtime approximation that evaluates the source method twice per derivative evaluation — only enable it for methods known to be pure (no side effects) and reasonably smooth near the evaluation point. public bool AllowNumericalFallback { get; } Property Value bool ParameterName Gets the name of the parameter that will be considered the differentiation variable. public string ParameterName { get; } Property Value string Methods Add(BinaryExpression, Expression, Expression) Applies the sum rule to differentiate the addition of two expressions. [ExpressionSignature(ExpressionType.Add)] protected Expression Add(BinaryExpression e, Expression left, Expression right) Parameters e BinaryExpression Addition expression to transform. left Expression Left operand of the addition. right Expression Right operand of the addition. Returns Expression The sum of the derivatives of the operands. Constant(ConstantExpression, object) Computes the derivative of a constant expression, which always yields zero. [ExpressionSignature(ExpressionType.Constant)] protected Expression Constant(ConstantExpression e, object value) Parameters e ConstantExpression Source constant expression. value object Value carried by the constant. Returns Expression A constant expression representing zero. Convert(UnaryExpression, Expression) Differentiates the wrapped operand and re-applies the conversion's declared result type when the derivative's type does not already match it, so the derivative expression stays type-consistent with the original conversion node. [ExpressionSignature(ExpressionType.Convert)] protected Expression Convert(UnaryExpression e, Expression operand) Parameters e UnaryExpression Conversion expression to transform. operand Expression Wrapped expression operand. Returns Expression The derivative of the wrapped operand, converted back to e.Type if needed. ConvertChecked(UnaryExpression, Expression) Differentiates the wrapped operand through a checked conversion. Checked conversions exist specifically to guard against narrowing/overflow, which has no well-defined symbolic derivative; only a trivial same-type checked conversion is passed through, anything else is rejected instead of silently stripped. [ExpressionSignature(ExpressionType.ConvertChecked)] protected Expression ConvertChecked(UnaryExpression e, Expression operand) Parameters e UnaryExpression Checked conversion expression to transform. operand Expression Wrapped expression operand. Returns Expression The derivative of the wrapped operand when the checked conversion is a same-type no-op. Exceptions NotSupportedException Thrown when the checked conversion actually changes the type (a narrowing or otherwise non-trivial conversion), since differentiating through it is not well-defined. Cos(MethodCallExpression, Expression) Differentiates a cosine call expression by applying the chain rule and negating the sine. [ExpressionCallSignature(typeof(double), "Cos")] protected Expression Cos(MethodCallExpression e, Expression operand) Parameters e MethodCallExpression Call expression describing the cosine invocation. operand Expression Operand of the cosine call. Returns Expression The derivative of the cosine expression. Derivate(LambdaExpression) Builds the derivative of the provided lambda expression with respect to the configured parameter. public Expression Derivate(LambdaExpression e) Parameters e LambdaExpression Lambda expression to differentiate. Returns Expression The simplified derivative expression. Exceptions InvalidOperationException Thrown when no parameter named ParameterName is found in e, or when more than one distinct parameter shares that name (an ambiguous match that cannot be resolved by name alone). Derivate(LambdaExpression, out bool) Builds the derivative of the provided lambda expression with respect to the configured parameter, additionally reporting whether the result is an exact symbolic derivative or includes at least one finite-difference approximation. public Expression Derivate(LambdaExpression e, out bool isExact) Parameters e LambdaExpression Lambda expression to differentiate. isExact bool true when every part of the result was produced by an exact symbolic rule; false when at least one sub-expression fell back to the numerical finite-difference approximation (only possible when AllowNumericalFallback is true, since otherwise an unknown method throws instead of approximating). Returns Expression The simplified derivative expression. Exceptions InvalidOperationException Thrown when this instance was constructed with a parameter name and no parameter named ParameterName is found in e, or more than one distinct parameter shares that name (an ambiguous match that cannot be resolved by name alone). When this instance was instead constructed with an exact ParameterExpression instance, thrown when that instance is not one of e's declared parameters. Divide(BinaryExpression, Expression, Expression) Applies the quotient rule to differentiate the division of two expressions. [ExpressionSignature(ExpressionType.Divide)] protected Expression Divide(BinaryExpression e, Expression left, Expression right) Parameters e BinaryExpression Division expression to transform. left Expression Dividend expression. right Expression Divisor expression. Returns Expression The derivative computed using the quotient rule. Exp(MethodCallExpression, Expression) Differentiates an exponential call expression by applying the chain rule. [ExpressionCallSignature(typeof(double), "Exp")] protected Expression Exp(MethodCallExpression e, Expression operand) Parameters e MethodCallExpression Call expression describing the exponential invocation. operand Expression Operand of the exponential call. Returns Expression The derivative of the exponential expression. FinalizeExpression(Expression, Expression[]) Handles method-call derivatives that are not covered by specific derivative rules. For single-argument double functions, this applies a centered finite-difference approximation and multiplies by the inner derivative. protected override Expression FinalizeExpression(Expression e, Expression[] parameters) Parameters e Expression Expression currently being finalized. parameters Expression[] Prepared child expressions. Returns Expression A derivative expression when supported. Log10(MethodCallExpression, Expression) Differentiates a base-10 logarithm call expression. [ExpressionCallSignature(typeof(double), "Log10")] protected Expression Log10(MethodCallExpression e, Expression operand) Parameters e MethodCallExpression Call expression describing the logarithm invocation. operand Expression Operand of the logarithm call. Returns Expression The derivative of the base-10 logarithm expression. LogMath(MethodCallExpression, Expression) Differentiates a natural logarithm call expression. [ExpressionCallSignature(typeof(double), "Log")] protected Expression LogMath(MethodCallExpression e, Expression operand) Parameters e MethodCallExpression Call expression describing the logarithm invocation. operand Expression Operand of the logarithm call. Returns Expression The derivative of the logarithm expression. Multiply(BinaryExpression, Expression, Expression) Applies the product rule to differentiate the multiplication of two expressions. [ExpressionSignature(ExpressionType.Multiply)] protected Expression Multiply(BinaryExpression e, Expression left, Expression right) Parameters e BinaryExpression Multiplication expression to transform. left Expression Left operand of the multiplication. right Expression Right operand of the multiplication. Returns Expression The derivative computed using the product rule. Negate(UnaryExpression, Expression) Applies the derivative to a negated expression following the chain rule. [ExpressionSignature(ExpressionType.Negate)] protected Expression Negate(UnaryExpression e, Expression operand) Parameters e UnaryExpression Negation expression to transform. operand Expression Operand of the negation. Returns Expression The derivative of the negated operand. Parameter(ParameterExpression) Computes the derivative of a parameter expression by comparing it to the differentiation variable. [ExpressionSignature(ExpressionType.Parameter)] protected Expression Parameter(ParameterExpression e) Parameters e ParameterExpression Source parameter expression. Returns Expression One when the parameter matches the configured variable; otherwise zero. Power(BinaryExpression, Expression, ConstantExpression) Differentiates a power expression with a constant exponent using the standard power rule. [ExpressionSignature(ExpressionType.Power)] protected Expression Power(BinaryExpression e, Expression left, ConstantExpression right) Parameters e BinaryExpression Power expression being transformed. left Expression Base expression. right ConstantExpression Constant exponent. Returns Expression The derivative computed by the power rule. Power(BinaryExpression, Expression, Expression) Differentiates a power expression that has an expression exponent by applying logarithmic differentiation. [ExpressionSignature(ExpressionType.Power)] protected Expression Power(BinaryExpression e, Expression left, Expression right) Parameters e BinaryExpression Power expression being transformed. left Expression Base expression. right Expression Exponent expression. Returns Expression The derivative computed through logarithmic differentiation. Remarks Logarithmic differentiation of f(x)^g(x) requires f(x) > 0: this is not an artificial narrowing introduced by using Log(f) here, since the original expression itself is already only real-valued (rather than NaN) for a negative base when the exponent is non-integer, and the exponent is a general (non-constant) expression here. Unlike the integration rules for 1/x or tan(x) (which use Log(Abs(x)) because the original expressions they replace remain well-defined for negative inputs), replacing Log(f) with Log(Abs(f)) here would not extend the domain of a valid symbolic derivative — it would just produce a different, unrelated formula. Sin(MethodCallExpression, Expression) Differentiates a sine call expression by applying the chain rule. [ExpressionCallSignature(typeof(double), "Sin")] protected Expression Sin(MethodCallExpression e, Expression operand) Parameters e MethodCallExpression Call expression describing the sine invocation. operand Expression Operand of the sine call. Returns Expression The derivative of the sine expression. Subtract(BinaryExpression, Expression, Expression) Applies the difference rule to differentiate the subtraction of two expressions. [ExpressionSignature(ExpressionType.Subtract)] protected Expression Subtract(BinaryExpression e, Expression left, Expression right) Parameters e BinaryExpression Subtraction expression to transform. left Expression Left operand of the subtraction. right Expression Right operand of the subtraction. Returns Expression The difference of the derivatives of the operands. Tan(MethodCallExpression, Expression) Differentiates a tangent call expression using the identity d/dx tan(f) = f'(x) / cos²(f(x)). [ExpressionCallSignature(typeof(double), "Tan")] protected Expression Tan(MethodCallExpression e, Expression operand) Parameters e MethodCallExpression Call expression describing the tangent invocation. operand Expression Operand of the tangent call. Returns Expression The derivative of the tangent expression.

Returns Expression The integral of the wrapped operand, converted back to e.Type if needed. ConvertChecked(UnaryExpression, Expression) Integrates the wrapped operand through a checked conversion. Checked conversions exist specifically to guard against narrowing/overflow, which has no well-defined symbolic integral; only a trivial same-type checked conversion is passed through, anything else is rejected instead of silently stripped. FinalizeExpression(Expression, Expression[]) Called when no integration rule matches the current node. Replaces the base transformer's generic Exception with a specific, node-tagged NotSupportedException, so a failed integration is distinguishable as "not integrable" (see TODO-2026-07-11-pass3.md item #42) and TryIntegrate<T>(LambdaExpression, string) can surface the offending node. protected override Expression FinalizeExpression(Expression e, Expression[] parameters) Parameters e Expression The expression that could not be integrated. parameters Expression[] Prepared child expressions (unused; no rule consumed them). api/Utils.Mathematics.Expressions.MathEx.summary: Class MathExpressionExtensions Namespace Utils.Mathematics.Expressions Assembly Utils.Mathematics.dll Provides helper methods to derive lambda expressions using ExpressionDerivation<T>. public static class MathExpressionExtensions Inheritance object MathExpressionExtensions Inherited Members object.Equals(object) object.Equals(object, object) object.GetHashCode() object.GetType() object.MemberwiseClone() object.ReferenceEquals(object, object) object.ToString() Methods Derivate(LambdaExpression) Computes the derivative of a single-parameter lambda expression with respect to its declared parameter. The scalar type is inferred from that parameter's CLR type instead of assuming double. public static LambdaExpression Derivate(this LambdaExpression e) Parameters e LambdaExpression Lambda expression to differentiate. Returns LambdaExpression A lambda expression representing the derivative. Derivate(LambdaExpression, ParameterExpression) Computes the derivative of a lambda expression with respect to the exact declared parameter instance. Unlike the name-based overload, this resolves the differentiation variable unambiguously even when the parameter's Name is null or shared with another, unrelated parameter (see TODO-pass4 item #47). The scalar type is inferred from parameter's own CLR type instead of assuming double. public static LambdaExpression Derivate(this LambdaExpression e, ParameterExpression parameter) Parameters e LambdaExpression Lambda expression to differentiate. parameter ParameterExpression The exact parameter instance used as the differentiation variable. Returns LambdaExpression A lambda expression representing the derivative. Exceptions NotSupportedException Thrown when parameter's CLR type does not implement IFloatingPoint<TSelf>. Derivate(LambdaExpression, string) Computes the derivative of a lambda expression with respect to the specified parameter name. The scalar type is inferred from that parameter's CLR type instead of assuming double. public static LambdaExpression Derivate(this LambdaExpression e, string paramName) Parameters e LambdaExpression Lambda expression to differentiate. paramName string Name of the parameter used as the differentiation variable. Returns LambdaExpression A lambda expression representing the derivative. Exceptions NotSupportedException Thrown when paramName's CLR type does not implement IFloatingPoint<TSelf>. Derivate<T>(LambdaExpression, ParameterExpression) Computes the derivative of a lambda expression with respect to the exact declared parameter instance. public static LambdaExpression Derivate<T>(this LambdaExpression e, ParameterExpression parameter) where T : IFloatingPoint<T> Parameters e LambdaExpression Lambda expression to differentiate. parameter ParameterExpression The exact parameter instance used as the differentiation variable. Returns LambdaExpression A lambda expression representing the derivative. Type Parameters T Floating-point type used by derivative rules. Derivate<T>(LambdaExpression, string) Computes the derivative of a lambda expression with respect to the specified parameter name. public static LambdaExpression Derivate<T>(this LambdaExpression e, string paramName) where T : IFloatingPoint<T> Parameters e LambdaExpression Lambda expression to differentiate. paramName string Name of the parameter used as the differentiation variable. Returns LambdaExpression A lambda expression representing the derivative. Type Parameters T Floating-point type used by derivative rules. Gradient(LambdaExpression) Computes the gradient of a multi-parameter lambda expression as an array of partial-derivative expressions. Each entry i is the partial derivative with respect to the i-th parameter. All parameters must share the same CLR type, which is inferred instead of assuming double. public static LambdaExpression[] Gradient(this LambdaExpression e) Parameters e LambdaExpression Lambda expression to differentiate. Returns LambdaExpression[] Array of lambda expressions representing each partial derivative; shares the same parameter list as e. Exceptions NotSupportedException Thrown when e's parameters do not share a single CLR type, or that type does not implement IFloatingPoint<TSelf>. Gradient<T>(LambdaExpression, params IEnumerable<ParameterExpression>) Computes the partial derivatives of a lambda expression with respect to the specified exact parameter instances. Unlike the name-based overload, this resolves each differentiation variable unambiguously even when multiple targeted parameters share one name — including null for unnamed parameters (see TODO-pass4 item #47). public static LambdaExpression[] Gradient<T>(this LambdaExpression e, params IEnumerable<ParameterExpression> parameters) where T : IFloatingPoint<T> Parameters e LambdaExpression Lambda expression to differentiate. parameters IEnumerable<ParameterExpression> The exact parameter instances to differentiate with respect to. Returns LambdaExpression[] Array of lambda expressions representing each partial derivative; shares the same parameter list as e. Type Parameters T Floating-point type used by derivative rules. Gradient<T>(LambdaExpression, params IEnumerable<string>) Computes the partial derivatives of a lambda expression with respect to the specified parameter names. public static LambdaExpression[] Gradient<T>(this LambdaExpression e, params IEnumerable<string> paramNames) where T : IFloatingPoint<T> Parameters e LambdaExpression Lambda expression to differentiate. paramNames IEnumerable<string> Names of the parameters to differentiate with respect to. api/Utils.Mathematics.Expressions.html.title: Namespace Utils.Mathematics.Expressions | omy.Utils Documentation
api/Utils.Mathematics.Expressions.html.summary: Namespace Utils.Mathematics.Expressions Classes ExpressionComparer Provides a custom equality comparer for Expression objects. The comparison involves simplifying each expression via an ExpressionSimplifier before checking for structural and semantic equivalence. ExpressionDerivation Provides the historical non-generic entry-point for double-based derivation. ExpressionDerivation<T> Provides single-variable symbolic differentiation for LINQ expression trees. ExpressionExtensions Provides extension methods for simplifying LINQ expression trees, as well as custom exceptions for extension-related errors. ExpressionExtensionsException Represents validation errors that occur while building derivative expressions. ExpressionIntegration Provides the historical non-generic entry-point for double-based integration. ExpressionIntegration<T> Provides integration rules for mathematical expression trees. ExpressionSimplifier Provides methods to simplify or rewrite expression trees by applying various transformations, typically related to arithmetic identities or constant folding. This partial class works alongside other parts of ExpressionSimplifier to compose a complete transformation pipeline. MathExpressionExtensions Provides helper methods to derive lambda expressions using ExpressionDerivation<T>. SymbolicParameterException Thrown when the symbolic transformation target parameter cannot be resolved from a LambdaExpression: the named parameter was not found, is present more than once (ambiguous), or the explicit parameter instance is not declared in the given lambda. This is a caller-error: InvalidInput classification in SymbolicTransformationResult. SymbolicTransformationResult A structured, non-throwing result of a symbolic transformation (differentiation or integration). Lets callers distinguish "not differentiable/integrable", "approximately transformed", "invalid input", and "internal construction failure" without relying on exception types or null conventions (see TODO-2026-07-11-pass3.md item #42). UnsupportedScalarOperationException Thrown when a required scalar math operation (e.g. Log, Sin, Sqrt) is not available for the scalar type used to build a symbolic derivative/integral. This is a specialized NotSupportedException so callers can categorize "operation unavailable for scalar type T" (see UnsupportedScalarOperation) distinctly from other unsupported-expression failures, while existing catch (NotSupportedException) sites keep working. Enums SymbolicTransformationStatus Categorizes the outcome of a non-throwing symbolic transformation (see TryDerivate<T>(LambdaExpression, string, bool) and TryIntegrate<T>(LambdaExpression, string)).

api/Utils.Mathematics.Fourier.FourierExt.summary: Class FourierExtensions Namespace Utils.Mathematics.Fourier Assembly Utils.Mathematics.dll Utility extension methods for Fourier-related computations. public static class FourierExtensions Inheritance object FourierExtensions Inherited Members object.Equals(object) object.Equals(object, object) object.GetHashCode() object.GetType() object.MemberwiseClone() object.ReferenceEquals(object, object) object.ToString() Methods GetAllBinFrequencies(Complex[], double) Returns the frequency represented by every bin of a Fourier transform result, including the negative (aliased) frequencies of the upper half - matching GetAmplitudes(Complex[])/ GetPhases(Complex[])'s bin count exactly, unlike GetFrequencies(Complex[], double) (see TODO-2026-07-11-pass4.md item #54). public static double[] GetAllBinFrequencies(this Complex[] transform, double sampleRate) Parameters transform Complex[] The transform result. Must be non-null and non-empty. sampleRate double Sampling rate in hertz. Must be finite and positive. Returns double[] Array of N frequencies in hertz: bin i < ceil(N/2) is the positive frequency i * sampleRate / N; bin i >= ceil(N/2) is the negative (aliased) frequency (i - N) * sampleRate / N - the same convention as e.g. NumPy's fftfreq, under which an even-length transform's Nyquist bin is reported as the negative Nyquist frequency. Exceptions ArgumentNullException Thrown when transform is null. ArgumentException Thrown when transform is empty. ArgumentOutOfRangeException Thrown when sampleRate is not finite or is not positive. GetAmplitudes(Complex[]) Returns the raw spectral magnitude (Magnitude) of each bin in the transform result - not a physically normalized sinusoidal amplitude (see TODO-2026-07-11-pass4.md item #55). Depending on the forward transform's own normalization convention, signal length, one-sided-vs-two-sided selection, and any window's coherent gain, the value that corresponds to a signal component's actual physical amplitude generally requires further scaling by the caller (e.g. this library's own forward transform is unnormalized, so a pure sinusoid's peak bin magnitude equals signal amplitude * N / 2, not the amplitude itself - see GetMagnitudes(Complex[])'s "constant signal" example). GetMagnitudes(Complex[]) is an identical, more accurately-named alias; this method is kept for backward compatibility. public static double[] GetAmplitudes(this Complex[] transform) Parameters transform Complex[] The transform result. Must be non-null and non-empty. Returns double[] Array of raw magnitudes, one per bin. Exceptions ArgumentNullException Thrown when transform is null. ArgumentException Thrown when transform is empty. GetFrequencies(Complex[], double) Returns the frequency represented by each bin in the first half of a Fourier transform result. public static double[] GetFrequencies(this Complex[] transform, double sampleRate) Parameters transform Complex[] The transform result. Must be non-null and non-empty. sampleRate double Sampling rate in hertz. Must be finite and positive. Returns double[] Array of N/2 frequencies in hertz, one per bin. Exceptions ArgumentNullException Thrown when transform is null. ArgumentException Thrown when transform is empty. ArgumentOutOfRangeException Thrown when sampleRate is not finite or is not positive (previously only sampleRate <= 0 was checked, silently accepting NaN - see TODO-2026-07-11-pass4.md item #53). GetMagnitudes(Complex[]) Returns the raw spectral magnitude (Magnitude) of each bin in the transform result. The more accurately-named counterpart to GetAmplitudes(Complex[]) (see TODO-2026-07-11-pass4.md item #55): "amplitude" suggests a physically normalized sinusoidal amplitude, but this is the raw, unnormalized FFT bin magnitude - e.g. for this library's unnormalized forward transform, the FFT of a constant signal of value 1 over N samples has a DC-bin magnitude of N, not 1. public static double[] GetMagnitudes(this Complex[] transform) Parameters transform Complex[] The transform result. Must be non-null and non-empty. Returns double[] Array of raw magnitudes, one per bin. Exceptions ArgumentNullException Thrown when transform is null. ArgumentException Thrown when transform is empty. api/Utils.Mathematics.Fourier.FourierWin.title: Class FourierWindow | omy.Utils Documentation
api/Utils.Mathematics.Fourier.FourierWin.summary: Class FourierWindow Namespace Utils.Mathematics.Fourier Assembly Utils.Mathematics.dll Provides standard spectral windowing functions for FFT preprocessing. public static class FourierWindow Inheritance object FourierWindow Inherited Members object.Equals(object) object.Equals(object, object) object.GetHashCode() object.GetType() object.MemberwiseClone() object.ReferenceEquals(object, object) object.ToString() Remarks Coefficient range: not every window is bounded to [0, 1]. Hann, Hamming, and Blackman are (modulo floating-point rounding noise of at most a few ulps right at the edges, where the ideal mathematical value is exactly zero) non-negative and never exceed 1. FlatTop<T>(int), however, is defined by IEC 61672 with coefficients that are negative by design near its edges; callers must not assume a shared [0, 1] range across every window in this class. Precision: every window is computed using double-precision trigonometry (Cos(double)) regardless of the requested output type T, then converted with T.CreateChecked. This is a deliberate choice, not an accidental precision leak: it gives every output type (including decimal, which has no ITrigonometricFunctions<TSelf> implementation and could not use a generic-precision cosine anyway) the same double-precision-accurate standard window coefficients, rather than constraining T to ITrigonometricFunctions<TSelf> and losing accuracy (and decimal support) for low-precision output types whose own trigonometric implementation would be no more accurate than double's in the first place. Methods Blackman<T>(int) Computes the Blackman window: w[n] = 0.42 − 0.5·cos(2πn/(N−1)) + 0.08·cos(4πn/(N−1)). Very low side-lobes (−58 dB); wider main lobe than Hann. Coefficients lie in [0, 1] (touching 0 at both edges) in exact arithmetic; floating-point rounding can produce a coefficient a few ulps below zero right at the edges. public static T[] Blackman<T>(int size) where T : struct, IFloatingPoint<T> Parameters size int Number of samples. Must be at least 2. Returns T[] Array of size window coefficients. Type Parameters T Floating-point output type. FlatTop<T>(int) Computes the Flat Top window (IEC 61672 coefficients): w[n] = 0.21557895 − 0.41663158·cos(2πn/N) + 0.277263158·cos(4πn/N) − 0.083578947·cos(6πn/N) + 0.006947368·cos(8πn/N). Excellent amplitude accuracy; broadest main lobe. Unlike the other windows in this class, coefficients are not confined to [0, 1]: the flat-top shape is negative by design near its edges (down to roughly −0.2). public static T[] FlatTop<T>(int size) where T : struct, IFloatingPoint<T> Parameters size int Number of samples. Must be at least 2. Returns T[] Array of size window coefficients. Type Parameters T Floating-point output type. Hamming<T>(int) Computes the Hamming window: w[n] = 0.54 − 0.46 · cos(2πn/(N−1)). Reduced side-lobe leakage compared to Hann; does not go to zero at the edges. Coefficients lie in [0.08, 1]. public static T[] Hamming<T>(int size) where T : struct, IFloatingPoint<T> Parameters size int Number of samples. Must be at least 2. Returns T[] Array of size window coefficients. Type Parameters T Floating-point output type. Hann<T>(int) Computes the Hann (Hanning) window: w[n] = 0.5 · (1 − cos(2πn/(N−1))). Good general-purpose window with −18 dB/octave roll-off. Coefficients lie in [0, 1] (touching 0 at both edges), modulo floating-point rounding noise. public static T[] Hann<T>(int size) where T : struct, IFloatingPoint<T> Parameters size int Number of samples. Must be at least 2. Returns T[] Array of size window coefficients. Type Parameters T Floating-point output type.
